company_persona: a consumer AI tutoring app used mostly by teenagers
title: The tutor that gives answers
prompt: You're the product lead for Marlo, an AI homework tutor whose users are mostly 13–17. Two things landed on your desk in the same week: a viral social thread showing that Marlo will simply hand students complete answers — parents and teachers are angry — and an internal log review showing a meaningful share of chats drifting into personal and emotional territory. The CEO asks you to own the response. What do you do?

hidden_facts.usage_split: About 60% of sessions are homework help. 22% of sessions drift off-topic at least once; roughly 6% are primarily personal/emotional conversations.
hidden_facts.age_profile: Median user age is 15. Age is self-reported at signup with no verification.
hidden_facts.answer_giving: In homework sessions, ~70% of interactions end with the student receiving the full answer within three turns. Time-in-app — the team's headline metric — correlates positively with answers given.
hidden_facts.retention_evidence: Students who get step-by-step guided help retain better at week 8 than answer-getters, but short-term engagement drops when answers are withheld.
hidden_facts.emotional_chats: There is no crisis-escalation flow. A small number of chats include self-harm mentions; today they get a generic safe-completion response with no resources, human review, or follow-up.
hidden_facts.district_pilots: Three school districts are piloting a classroom version — about 15% of the revenue roadmap. Two have threatened to cancel over the viral thread.
hidden_facts.competitor: The fastest-growing rival openly markets 'instant answers' and is growing faster than Marlo.
hidden_facts.regulatory: Scrutiny of minors' use of AI chat products is rising; a state attorney general letter about AI companions and minors is circulating in the industry.
hidden_facts.parent_sentiment: App-store reviews from parents increasingly say 'it does the homework for them'.
hidden_facts.expert_access: The company has budget approval to engage adolescent-psychology and crisis-response experts but hasn't done so.

ideal_answer_notes: Split the two problems immediately: pedagogy (answer-giving) and safety (emotional drift with no crisis flow) harm different people, carry different urgency, and have different fixes. Safety is first and non-negotiable.

Safety plan with concrete mechanics: a crisis classifier tuned for recall on self-harm signals, an escalation flow (age-appropriate resources, human review queue, follow-up), expert consultation before shipping, and audit sampling to measure the miss rate — not a policy paragraph.
Treat under-16 experience as its own design problem given self-reported age: safer defaults for everyone in ambiguous cases, and honest acknowledgment that age assurance is weak.

Name the metric conflict out loud: time-in-app rewards answer-giving. Replace it with an outcome-aligned north star (guided-session share, week-8 retention, learning outcomes in district pilots). The week-8 retention fact means the mission-aligned move is also the commercially rational one — say so.
Pedagogy fix: default to Socratic step-by-step scaffolding with friction before 'show the answer', a visible-to-teacher mode for classroom use, and framing for students about why.

Refuse the competitor trap with reasoning: chasing the 'instant answers' rival concedes the trust position; the district channel — 15% of the roadmap and currently threatening to cancel — only monetizes if trust holds.

Stakeholder plan: students (experience change comms), parents (visibility options, honest messaging), districts (briefing, teacher dashboard commitments), regulators (documented safety process before it's demanded).
Sequence explicitly: crisis flow hotfixed now; guided-by-default as an experiment next (measure the short-term engagement dip against week-8 retention); public comms alongside, not after.

Metrics and guardrails: guided-session share, week-8 retention, escalation precision/recall from audits, parent NPS, district renewals; watch for over-triggering (false escalations erode teen trust too).
extra_checklist: Separates the safety incident from the pedagogy problem and sequences safety first
Replaces time-in-app with an outcome-aligned success metric and defends the engagement tradeoff with the retention evidence
Designs a concrete crisis-escalation flow — classifier, resources, human review, audit-measured miss rate — rather than a policy statement

anchors.strong: Splits safety from pedagogy in the first minute, ships a real crisis flow with experts and audit metrics, calls out the time-in-app metric as the root incentive problem and replaces it, defends guided-by-default with the week-8 data against the engagement dip, and handles districts, parents, and regulators with a sequenced plan.
anchors.at_bar: Recognizes both problems and proposes reasonable fixes — hints at guided mode and some safety response — but needs prompting to sequence safety first, to make the escalation flow concrete, or to confront the engagement-metric conflict directly.
anchors.weak: Treats it as a PR problem, optimizes for keeping engagement against the 'instant answers' rival, proposes a filter or disclaimer as the entire safety answer, and never mentions the metric conflict, experts, or the district stakeholders.
